problem_addressed: Building generally capable embodied agents that can continuously explore, plan, and develop new skills in open-ended worlds without human intervention
core_claim: VOYAGER demonstrates that a three-component system combining an automatic curriculum, skill library, and iterative prompting mechanism can achieve open-ended lifelong learning in Minecraft
key_contributions: First LLM-powered embodied lifelong learning agent in Minecraft
Three-component architecture enabling continuous exploration and skill acquisition
Demonstrates superior performance (3.3× items, 2.3× distance, 15.3× speed) over baselines
novelty: Unlike prior Minecraft agents that rely on curated tasks or human interaction, VOYAGER uses a curiosity-driven automatic curriculum and self-verification to enable truly open-ended exploration without human intervention
method_summary: VOYAGER employs GPT-4 via blackbox queries to generate executable code for Minecraft actions, organized into a three-component system: an automatic curriculum for exploration, a skill library for storing behaviors, and an iterative prompting mechanism that refines code using environment feedback, execution errors, and self-verification
method_components: Automatic curriculum that maximizes exploration through curiosity-driven task selection
Ever-growing skill library of executable code for storing and retrieving complex behaviors
Iterative prompting mechanism incorporating environment feedback, execution errors, and self-verification
key_innovation: The iterative prompting mechanism that integrates environment feedback, execution errors, and self-verification to continuously improve generated code without model fine-tuning
assumptions: GPT-4's coding capabilities are sufficient for generating correct Minecraft actions
Self-verification can accurately assess task completion
Automatic curriculum can propose achievable tasks
theoretical_grounding: No formal theoretical proofs provided; empirical validation through ablation studies and comparative benchmarks
unstated_weaknesses: The paper focuses on Minecraft, which is far from medical image classification
The agent relies on GPT-4, which may have limitations in specialized domains
The paper doesn't discuss computational efficiency or resource requirements
The blackbox approach to LLM interaction might limit fine-grained control
threats_to_validity: The Minecraft environment might not generalize to real-world scenarios
The agent's performance in Minecraft might not translate to medical image tasks
The paper doesn't discuss potential biases in the LLM's responses
open_questions: How would this approach adapt to medical image classification?
Can the calibration-aware active learning principles be integrated into this framework?
How would the agent handle the high-stakes nature of medical diagnosis?
future_work: Extending to other domains beyond Minecraft
Adapting the methodology to specialized domains like medical imaging
gaps_this_enables: This paper could inspire new approaches to active learning in medical imaging
The compositional skill approach might be adapted for medical image classification pipelines
The iterative prompting mechanism could be used to improve model calibration
relevance_to_topic: While the paper is about Minecraft and not medical imaging, the methodology for active learning and skill acquisition could potentially be adapted to medical image classification.
reusable_assets: The code for the agent's framework
title: Voyager: An Open-Ended Embodied Agent with Large Language Models
